46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.

13	# The augmented Čech complex is exact (blueprint `lem:cech_augmented_resolution`)
14	
15	This file proves that the augmented Čech complex `cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F` is exact
16	(every homology object is the zero object in `X.Modules`) whenever `𝒰` is a finite
17	affine open cover of `X` with all pairwise intersections affine (ensured by separatedness
18	of `X`) and `F` is a quasi-coherent `O_X`-module.
19	
20	The theorem is placed here rather than in `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` to avoid an import
21	cycle: every ingredient of the sections/sheafification proof route
22	(`PresheafOfModules.homologyIsoSheafify`, `sectionCech_homology_exact_of_localizationAway`,
23	`affineCoverSystem`, `qcoh_iso_tilde_sections`) lives in a file that transitively imports
24	`CechHigherDirectImage.lean`.  Placing `cechAugmented_exact` in this downstream file
25	(`CechAugmentedResolution.lean`) makes all those ingredients available without a cycle.
26	The pure-Mathlib site lemmas (`isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_W`,
27	`isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_W_isZero`, `isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_isLocallyBijective`)
28	live in `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` and are importable here.

30	Blueprint: `lem:cech_augmented_resolution`.
31	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
32	  `lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`.

35	universe u

37	open CategoryTheory Limits
38	
39	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
40	
41	open Scheme.Modules
42	
43	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
44	
45	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — reflecting `IsZero` through a faithful functor -/
46	
47	/-- **A faithful functor that preserves zero morphisms reflects the zero object.**
48	If `F` is faithful and preserves zero morphisms and `F.obj X` is a zero object, then
49	`X` is a zero object. (Three-line proof off `IsZero.iff_id_eq_zero` +
50	`Functor.map_injective`; lighter than `reflects_exact_of_faithful`, which needs
51	`[Abelian]`.) Project-local Mathlib supplement (category theory). -/
52	lemma isZero_of_faithful_preservesZeroMorphisms
53	    {C D : Type*} [Category C] [Category D] [HasZeroMorphisms C] [HasZeroMorphisms D]
54	    (F : C ⥤ D) [F.PreservesZeroMorphisms] [F.Faithful] {Y : C}
55	    (h : IsZero (F.obj Y)) : IsZero Y := by
56	  rw [IsZero.iff_id_eq_zero] at h ⊢
57	  apply F.map_injective
58	  rw [F.map_id, F.map_zero]
59	  exact h
60	
61	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — a null-homotopic identity kills homology -/
62	
63	/-- **A complex homotopy-equivalent to zero has vanishing homology.**
64	If the identity of a homological complex `D` is homotopic to the zero map
65	(`Homotopy (𝟙 D) 0`), then every homology object `D.homology p` is a zero object.
66	This is the categorical packaging of the contracting-homotopy criterion: the
67	prepend-`i_fix` homotopy on the section {\v C}ech complex produces exactly such a
68	`Homotopy (𝟙 D) 0`, and this lemma converts it into the required
69	`IsZero (D.homology p)`.
70	
71	The proof is the standard three-lemma combo: homotopy-invariance of the induced
72	homology map (`Homotopy.homologyMap_eq`), the identity/zero computations
73	(`homologyMap_id`, `homologyMap_zero`), and the "identity equals zero" criterion for a
74	zero object (`IsZero.iff_id_eq_zero`).  Project-local Mathlib supplement (homological
75	algebra); blueprint `lem:cech_augmented_resolution`, formalization mechanism Step 3(d). -/
76	lemma isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero {C : Type*} [Category C] [Preadditive C]
77	    {ι : Type*} {c : ComplexShape ι} (D : HomologicalComplex C c) (p : ι)
78	    [D.HasHomology p] (ho : Homotopy (𝟙 D) 0) : IsZero (D.homology p) := by
79	  refine (IsZero.iff_id_eq_zero _).mpr ?_
80	  rw [← HomologicalComplex.homologyMap_id, ho.homologyMap_eq p, HomologicalComplex.homologyMap_zero]
81	
82	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — sheafification of a locally-zero presheaf -/
83	
84	open Opposite in
85	/-- **The sheafification of a presheaf that is locally a zero object vanishes.**
86	If for every object `U` of the site there is a covering sieve `S ∈ J U` all of whose
87	members `g : V ⟶ U` land on an object `V` where `Q.obj (op V)` is a zero object, then
88	the sheafification of `Q` is the zero object.
89	
90	This packages the three imported site lemmas
91	(`isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_isLocallyBijective`) into the "locally zero" form the
92	{\v C}ech-resolution argument consumes: the presheaf homology `V ↦ Ȟᵖ(V, F)` is a zero
93	object over every `V` contained in a cover member, and those `V` are a covering basis.
94	The local injectivity of `0 : Q ⟶ Z` (`Z` the constant zero presheaf) comes from the
95	hypothesis (each member's section group is a subsingleton); the local surjectivity is
96	free (the target is a zero object). Project-local Mathlib supplement (site theory). -/
97	lemma isZero_presheafToSheaf_of_locally_isZero {C : Type*} [Category C]
98	    (J : GrothendieckTopology C) [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{u}]
99	    [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat.{u}]
100	    {Q : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{u}}
101	    (h : ∀ U : C, ∃ S : Sieve U, S ∈ J U ∧
102	      ∀ ⦃V : C⦄ ⦃g : V ⟶ U⦄, S.arrows g → IsZero (Q.obj (op V))) :
103	    IsZero ((presheafToSheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u}).obj Q) := by
104	  let Z : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{u} := (Functor.const Cᵒᵖ).obj (AddCommGrpCat.of PUnit)
105	  have hZobj : ∀ x : Cᵒᵖ, IsZero (Z.obj x) := by
106	    intro x
107	    change IsZero (AddCommGrpCat.of PUnit)
108	    exact AddCommGrpCat.isZero_of_subsingleton _
109	  have hZ : IsZero Z := (Functor.isZero_iff Z).mpr hZobj
110	  haveI hli : Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective J (0 : Q ⟶ Z) := by
111	    refine ⟨?_⟩
112	    intro X x y _
113	    obtain ⟨S, hS, hSzero⟩ := h X.unop
114	    apply J.superset_covering _ hS
115	    intro V g hg
116	    change (ConcreteCategory.hom (Q.map g.op)) x = (ConcreteCategory.hom (Q.map g.op)) y
117	    haveI := AddCommGrpCat.subsingleton_of_isZero (hSzero hg)
118	    exact Subsingleton.elim _ _
119	  haveI hls : Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective J (0 : Q ⟶ Z) := by
120	    refine ⟨?_⟩
121	    intro U s
122	    refine J.superset_covering (fun V g _ => ?_) (J.top_mem U)
123	    refine ⟨0, ?_⟩
124	    haveI : Subsingleton (Z.obj (op V)) := AddCommGrpCat.subsingleton_of_isZero (hZobj (op V))
125	    exact Subsingleton.elim _ _
126	  exact CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_isLocallyBijective
127	    J (0 : Q ⟶ Z) hZ

129	/- Planner strategy:
130	Route: sections + sheafification (NOT stalks — `SheafOfModules.stalk` is absent from Mathlib).
131	Step 1: reflect `IsZero (homology p)` through the faithful additive forgetful functor
132	`SheafOfModules.toSheaf` (it preserves zero morphisms, so it reflects the zero object). Step 2:
133	the homology SHEAF = sheafification of the PRESHEAF homology, via the project engine
134	`PresheafOfModules.homologyIsoSheafify` (HigherDirectImagePresheaf.lean). Step 3: the presheaf
135	homology is `V ↦ Ȟᵖ(V,F)`, locally zero on the affine basis — over each basic affine `D(g) ⊆ Uᵢ`,
136	`qcoh_iso_tilde_sections` gives `F|_{D(g)} ≅ ~M` and

7139	[Sheafification kills a presheaf that is locally bijective to a locally-zero one]
7140	  \label{lem:sheafify_kills_locally_zero}
7141	  \lean{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_W,

7144	  \textit{Project-bespoke site-theory lemma (pure Mathlib site theory).}
7145	  Let \(J\) be a Grothendieck topology on a site \(\mathcal{C}\) and let \(J.W\) denote the
7146	  class of \emph{locally bijective} maps of presheaves --- the local-isomorphism class for
7147	  \(J\), i.e.\ the maps inverted by sheafification. Then:
7148	  \begin{enumerate}
7149	    \item Sheafification \(\operatorname{presheafToSheaf}\) carries every map in \(J.W\) to an
7150	      isomorphism; consequently it sends a presheaf that is locally bijective to a presheaf
7151	      with zero sheafification to a presheaf with zero sheafification. Precisely, if
7152	      \(\varphi : P \to Q\) lies in \(J.W\) and the sheafification of \(Q\) is the zero
7153	      sheaf, then the sheafification of \(P\) is the zero sheaf.
7154	    \item In particular, if \(P\) admits a locally bijective map to the zero presheaf --- for
7155	      instance if \(P\) is locally zero, i.e.\ vanishes on a basis of the topology --- then
7156	      the sheafification of \(P\) is the zero sheaf.
7157	    \item For an abelian-valued presheaf the locally-bijective hypothesis is the conjunction
7158	      of local injectivity and local surjectivity of the comparison map; a map that is both
7159	      locally injective and locally surjective lies in \(J.W\).
7160	  \end{enumerate}
7161	\end{lemma}
7162	\begin{proof}
7163	  Sheafification \(\operatorname{presheafToSheaf} : \widehat{\mathcal{C}} \to
7164	  \operatorname{Sh}(J)\) inverts exactly the maps of the class \(J.W\): by definition,
7165	  \(\varphi \in J.W\) if and only if \(\operatorname{presheafToSheaf}(\varphi)\) is an
7166	  isomorphism. Hence for \(\varphi : P \to Q\) in \(J.W\) the induced map
7167	  \(\operatorname{sheafify}(P) \to \operatorname{sheafify}(Q)\) is an isomorphism, and
7168	  being a zero object transports along an isomorphism: if \(\operatorname{sheafify}(Q)\)
7169	  is zero then so is \(\operatorname{sheafify}(P)\). This proves (1); part (2) is the
7170	  special case \(Q = 0\) (the zero presheaf, whose sheafification is the zero sheaf),
7171	  and a presheaf that vanishes on a basis admits such a locally bijective map to \(0\).
7172	  For (3), sheafification is additive, and a map of abelian presheaves that is
7173	  simultaneously locally injective and locally surjective is a local isomorphism,
7174	  i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\); applying (1) then collapses its sheafification.
7175	\end{proof}
